summary: Gruber responds to Tim Bray's argument that the iPhone needs a hardware keyboard, firmly disagreeing. He argues that all phone-sized keyboards are poor compared to full-size keyboards, but that most people can thumb-type just as well on an iPhone as on a BlackBerry or slider phone after a brief adjustment period. He frames Apple's refusal to add a hardware keyboard as part of the broader 'Apple Way' — making products that many people love rather than products that all people like, even at the cost of criticism. He draws parallels to Apple's commitment to non-removable batteries across its product line.
key_points: All phone-sized keyboards — hardware or software — are poor compared to full-size keyboards, so the real question is relative thumb-typing speed, not absolute quality
After a week or two of adjustment, most people type just as well on an iPhone's software keyboard as on hardware-keyboard phones
Apple's strategy is to make products many people love rather than products all people like, which requires being unafraid of breaking conventions
The people who struggle most with the iPhone keyboard are those with the most hardware keyboard muscle memory — and the reverse is equally true
Trying to please everyone by adding every demanded feature produces something like Windows that appeals to no one with taste
money_quotes: Apple tries to make things that many people love, not things that all people like.
The key is that they're not afraid of the staunch criticism, and often outright derision, that comes with breaking conventions.

Even if you try to make something that appeals to everyone by adding every single clamored-for feature, you wind up with something like Windows that does not appeal to people with a taste for the elegant and refined.
That the iPhone — or specifically its software touchscreen keyboard — does not appeal to everyone is not a problem. Nothing appeals to everyone.
My argument is not that the iPhone keyboard is poor compared to hardware phone keyboards but that it's an acceptable trade-off.
The people who seem to struggle the most with the iPhone keyboard are those who've spent the most time thumb-typing with hardware keyboards.
key_insight: Apple's refusal to add a hardware keyboard to the iPhone isn't a deficiency but a deliberate design philosophy: making something many people love is more valuable than making something everyone finds acceptable.
